items.explanation:
Capital since 1846; not the largest city (Birmingham is larger)
Known as 'Cradle of the Confederacy' — the first Confederate capital before it moved to Richmond
Rosa Parks refused to give up her bus seat here (December 1, 1955), sparking the Montgomery Bus Boycott

Only U.S. state capital not accessible by road — no highway connects it to the rest of Alaska
Became capital of Alaska Territory in 1906; retained at statehood (1959)
Anchorage is ~10 times larger; Juneau's population is about 32,000

Capital since Arizona achieved statehood in 1912; largest state capital by population (~1.6 million)
One of the few state capitals that is also the state's largest city and a top-10 U.S. city
Built on ruins of ancient Hohokam settlements; named for the mythological bird rising from ashes

Capital since 1821 (territorial capital); Arkansas achieved statehood in 1836
Site of the 1957 Little Rock Central High School desegregation crisis — Eisenhower sent Army troops to enforce integration
Named for a small rocky outcropping (French: 'la petite roche') on the Arkansas River

State capital since 1854; not the largest city (Los Angeles is ~15 times larger)
Gold Rush supply hub; western terminus of the first Transcontinental Railroad (1869)
San Jose and San Francisco were both briefly the capital before Sacramento

Capital since Colorado achieved statehood in 1876; the largest city in Colorado
Known as the 'Mile High City' — the State Capitol's west steps are exactly 5,280 feet above sea level
Founded as a supply camp during the Pike's Peak Gold Rush (1858)

Sole capital since 1875 (previously co-capital with New Haven)
Known as the 'Insurance Capital of the World' — major insurers have been headquartered here since the 19th century
Mark Twain lived in Hartford for 17 years (1874–1891) and wrote Huckleberry Finn and Tom Sawyer here

Capital since 1777; moved from New Castle to reduce British access during the Revolutionary War
Not the largest city (Wilmington is larger)
Delaware was first to ratify the U.S. Constitution (December 7, 1787), earning the nickname 'The First State'

Capital since 1824; chosen as a compromise midpoint between Pensacola and St. Augustine
Not the largest city (Jacksonville has the largest land area of any U.S. city in the contiguous states; Miami metro is larger)
Home of Florida State University and Florida A&M University

Capital since 1868; previously Milledgeville was the capital
Burned by General Sherman during his March to the Sea (November 1864); rebuilt and became the South's commercial hub
Birthplace of Martin Luther King Jr. (1929); headquarters of Coca-Cola, CNN, Delta Air Lines, and the CDC

Capital of the Kingdom, Territory, and State of Hawaii; Hawaii achieved statehood in 1959
Only U.S. state capital located outside North America (geographically in Polynesia, on the island of Oahu)
Site of Pearl Harbor (attacked December 7, 1941); birthplace of President Barack Obama (1961)

Capital since Idaho achieved statehood in 1890; the largest city in Idaho
Derived from French 'boisé' meaning 'wooded' — named by French-Canadian fur trappers for the trees along the Boise River
One of the fastest-growing U.S. cities in the 2010s due to tech industry migration

Capital since 1837; moved from Vandalia largely due to Abraham Lincoln's political maneuvering in the state legislature
Not the largest city (Chicago is approximately 50 times larger)
Lincoln's home from 1837 until he left for Washington in 1861; his presidential library and tomb are here

Capital since Indiana achieved statehood in 1816; purpose-built as the state capital
Largest city in Indiana; name combines 'Indiana' + 'polis' (Greek for city)
Home of the Indianapolis 500, held annually since 1911 at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway

Capital since Iowa achieved statehood in 1846; the largest city in Iowa
National hub for the insurance industry — several major insurers are headquartered here
Iowa holds the first presidential caucuses every four years, giving Des Moines outsized political importance

Capital since Kansas achieved statehood in 1861; not the largest city (Wichita is larger)
Brown v. Board of Education (1954) originated here — the Topeka school district's segregation was the case that reached the Supreme Court
Terminus of the Santa Fe Trail; a major railroad hub in the 19th century

Capital since Kentucky achieved statehood in 1792; one of the smallest state capitals in the U.S. (~28,000 people)
Neither the largest (Louisville) nor second-largest (Lexington) city in the state
Located in the heart of Kentucky Bourbon Country along the Kentucky River

Capital since 1880; moved from New Orleans following Reconstruction-era political disputes
French for 'red stick' — named for a red cypress pole that marked tribal territorial boundaries
Louisiana State University is located here; home of Huey Long, the controversial 1930s governor and senator

Capital since Maine achieved statehood in 1820 (separated from Massachusetts)
Smallest state capital east of the Mississippi River by population (~19,000)
Not the largest city (Portland is larger); Augusta sits on the Kennebec River

Capital since 1694 — one of the oldest continuously serving state capitals in the U.S.
The U.S. Congress met here 1783–1784; the Treaty of Paris (formally ending the Revolutionary War) was ratified here on January 14, 1784
Not the largest city (Baltimore is ~10 times larger); home of the U.S. Naval Academy (founded 1845)

Capital since the Massachusetts Bay Colony (1630); one of the oldest cities in the U.S.
Site of the Boston Massacre (1770), Boston Tea Party (1773), and the start of the Revolutionary War at Lexington and Concord
Home of Harvard University (1636), MIT, and over 50 colleges; one of the few capitals that is also the state's largest city

Capital since 1847; moved from Detroit because its border location made it vulnerable to British or Canadian attack
Not the largest city (Detroit is ~5 times larger)
Home of Michigan State University in nearby East Lansing; birthplace of the Oldsmobile (1897)

Capital since Minnesota achieved statehood in 1858
Not the largest city — Minneapolis (the 'Twin City') is slightly larger
Saint Paul and Minneapolis form the 'Twin Cities' metropolitan area, one of the most educated metros in the U.S.

Capital since 1822; named after Andrew Jackson
Largest city in Mississippi
A major civil rights battleground in the 1960s — site of the Jackson Woolworth's lunch counter sit-in (1963) and the murder of NAACP leader Medgar Evers (1963)

Capital since Missouri achieved statehood in 1821; named after Thomas Jefferson
Not the largest city (Kansas City and St. Louis are both much larger); Jefferson City's population is ~43,000
Located on the Missouri River; considered a gateway to westward expansion

Capital since Montana achieved statehood in 1889; not the largest city (Billings is ~4 times larger)
Founded as 'Last Chance Gulch' during the 1864 gold rush; later renamed Helena after Helena, Minnesota
Produced more millionaires per capita during the Gold Rush than almost anywhere else in the U.S.

Capital since Nebraska achieved statehood in 1867; named after Abraham Lincoln
Not the largest city (Omaha is roughly twice as large)
Home of the University of Nebraska; William Jennings Bryan lived and is buried here

Capital since Nevada achieved statehood in 1864; named after frontiersman Kit Carson
Smallest state capital in the contiguous U.S. by population (~58,000)
Not the largest city (Las Vegas is ~30 times larger; Reno is also larger)

Capital since New Hampshire ratified the Constitution in 1788 (the 9th and decisive state)
Not the largest city (Manchester is larger)
The Concord Coach stagecoach — manufactured here — was the dominant overland passenger vehicle in 19th-century America

Capital since New Jersey was among the original 13 states; capital since 1790
Not the largest city (Newark is larger)
Battle of Trenton (December 26, 1776): Washington crossed the Delaware and defeated Hessian forces — a turning point of the Revolution

Founded by Spanish colonists in 1610 — the oldest state capital in the U.S.
Not the largest city (Albuquerque is ~4 times larger)
At 7,199 feet elevation, the highest state capital in the U.S.; the Palace of the Governors (built 1610) is the oldest continuously occupied public building in the U.S.

Capital since New York ratified the Constitution (1788); Albany has served as capital since 1797
Not the largest city (New York City is ~170 times larger)
Eastern terminus of the Erie Canal (completed 1825); a crucial 19th-century commercial and political hub

Capital since North Carolina ratified the Constitution in 1789; named after Sir Walter Raleigh
Not the largest city (Charlotte is larger)
Raleigh, Durham, and Chapel Hill form the 'Research Triangle' — one of the largest tech and research clusters in the U.S.

Capital since North Dakota achieved statehood in 1889; not the largest city (Fargo is larger)
Named after German Chancellor Otto von Bismarck — Northern Pacific Railroad hoped to attract German investment
Sits on the Missouri River near the geographic center of North America

Capital since Ohio achieved statehood in 1803; Columbus has been capital since 1816
Largest city in Ohio — surpassed Cleveland and Cincinnati in the mid-20th century
Home of Ohio State University, one of the largest universities in the U.S. by enrollment (~60,000 students)

Capital since 1910; the only U.S. state capital where the city's name contains the state's name
Site of the Oklahoma City bombing (April 19, 1995) — deadliest domestic terrorist attack in U.S. history before 9/11
Largest city in Oklahoma; home to the National Cowboy and Western Heritage Museum

Capital since Oregon achieved statehood in 1859; not the largest city (Portland is ~3 times larger)
Endpoint of the Oregon Trail; Salem was a major agricultural center in the Willamette Valley
Named after Salem, Massachusetts; settled by Methodist missionaries in the 1840s

Capital since 1812; moved from Lancaster (briefly from Philadelphia before that)
Not the largest city (Philadelphia is ~15 times larger; Pittsburgh is also larger)
Three Mile Island nuclear accident (March 28, 1979) occurred on the Susquehanna River near Harrisburg — worst U.S. commercial nuclear accident

Capital since Rhode Island ratified the Constitution in 1790; co-capital with Newport until 1900
Founded by Roger Williams in 1636 after he was expelled from Massachusetts Bay Colony for religious dissent
Largest city in Rhode Island; home to Brown University (Ivy League) and RISD

Capital since 1786; moved from Charleston to a more central location
Largest city in South Carolina
Sherman burned Columbia in February 1865 during the final campaigns of the Civil War; the State House still bears marks from Confederate cannon fire

Capital since South Dakota achieved statehood in 1889; second-smallest state capital by population (~14,000)
Not the largest city (Sioux Falls is ~10 times larger)
Pronounced 'PEER', not 'pee-AIR'; located near the geographic center of South Dakota on the Missouri River

Capital since Tennessee achieved statehood in 1796; the largest city in Tennessee
Country music capital of the world — home of the Grand Ole Opry (founded 1925) and Music Row
Consolidated city-county government with Davidson County since 1963; home to Vanderbilt University

Capital since the Republic of Texas (1839); retained when Texas joined the Union (1845)
Not the largest city (Houston is ~3 times larger); Austin is the 4th-largest city in Texas
Home of the University of Texas; major tech hub since the 1990s and one of the fastest-growing U.S. cities

Capital since Utah Territory (1850); Utah achieved statehood in 1896
Founded in 1847 by Brigham Young and Mormon (LDS) pioneers who arrived from Illinois and Iowa
Headquarters of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints; hosted the 2002 Winter Olympics

Capital since Vermont achieved statehood in 1791 (first state added after the original 13)
Smallest state capital in the United States by population (~8,000 people)
Spelled 'Montpelier' (one 'l'), not 'Montpellier' like the French city; the only U.S. state capital without a McDonald's

Capital since 1780; moved from Williamsburg to be less vulnerable to British naval attack
Also served as capital of the Confederate States of America (1861–1865)
Fell to Union forces April 3, 1865 — six days before Lee's surrender at Appomattox
Not the largest city (Virginia Beach is larger by population)

Capital since Washington Territory (1853); retained when Washington achieved statehood in 1889
Not the largest city (Seattle is ~18 times larger)
Named for the nearby Olympic Mountains; located at the southern tip of Puget Sound

Capital since West Virginia separated from Virginia in 1863 during the Civil War; became a state June 20, 1863
Largest city in West Virginia; not to be confused with Charleston, South Carolina
West Virginia was the only state created by seceding from a Confederate state — made possible because the western counties opposed secession

Capital since Wisconsin achieved statehood in 1848; named after President James Madison
Not the largest city (Milwaukee is larger)
Home of the University of Wisconsin–Madison and the 'Wisconsin Idea' — that the university's mission is to serve the entire state

Capital since Wyoming Territory (1869); Wyoming achieved statehood in 1890
Largest city in Wyoming; founded in 1867 as a Union Pacific Railroad town
Wyoming was the first U.S. territory to grant women full voting rights (1869) — 50 years before the 19th Amendment
